title: Neurogenetics Comprehensive Examination
description: 60-question board-style assessment covering all curriculum modules

questions.question: A 3-day-old term infant presents with tonic seizures and burst-suppression on EEG. No family history of epilepsy. Metabolic workup is normal. Exome sequencing reveals a de novo heterozygous missense variant in KCNQ2 classified as likely pathogenic. Which anticonvulsant is most appropriate for this infant?
options: Phenobarbital, the standard first-line agent for neonatal seizures regardless of etiology
Carbamazepine or phenytoin, because KCNQ2 encephalopathy responds preferentially to sodium channel blockade
Levetiracetam, a broad-spectrum agent with a favorable neonatal safety profile
Vigabatrin, the recommended first-line for all epileptic encephalopathies with burst suppression
questions.explanation: KCNQ2 encodes the Kv7.2 potassium channel subunit. Loss-of-function variants cause neonatal epileptic encephalopathy by reducing the M-current, the normal brake on neuronal excitability. Sodium channel blockers (carbamazepine, phenytoin) are uniquely effective for KCNQ2 encephalopathy and should be prioritized. This is a paradigm of precision medicine in genetic epilepsy.

questions.question: A 7-month-old infant presents with recurrent febrile seizures at temperatures above 38 degrees Celsius, temperature sensitivity, and developmental plateau. Genetic testing reveals a heterozygous SCN1A frameshift variant classified as pathogenic (PVS1 + PM2 + PP4). Which class of medications should be avoided?
options: Benzodiazepines, due to paradoxical excitation risk in Dravet syndrome
Valproate, due to hepatotoxicity risk in SCN1A-positive patients
Sodium channel blockers (oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, carbamazepine, phenytoin), which worsen seizures by further reducing Nav1.1 activity in inhibitory interneurons
Levetiracetam, which increases sodium channel activity and worsens Dravet
questions.explanation: SCN1A haploinsufficiency reduces Nav1.1 activity preferentially in GABAergic inhibitory interneurons, causing Dravet syndrome. Sodium channel blockers further reduce Nav1.1 function and are contraindicated — they can precipitate status epilepticus. Valproate, clobazam, and stiripentol are preferred agents.

questions.question: A toddler with drug-resistant epilepsy has a CSF glucose of 25 mg/dL with simultaneous serum glucose of 90 mg/dL (CSF/serum ratio 0.28). Which treatment and genetic mechanism are most appropriate?
options: Ketogenic diet; SLC2A1 haploinsufficiency impairs glucose transport across the blood-brain barrier, and ketone bodies provide an alternative brain fuel
Pyridoxine supplementation; ALDH7A1 deficiency causes pyridoxal-5-phosphate depletion
Empiric antibiotics; hypoglycorrhachia of this degree suggests bacterial meningitis
High-carbohydrate diet to maximize glucose delivery to the brain
questions.explanation: A CSF/serum glucose ratio <0.4 (normal >=0.6) in the absence of CNS infection indicates GLUT1 deficiency syndrome (SLC2A1 haploinsufficiency). The blood-brain barrier glucose transporter is deficient, starving the brain of its primary fuel. The ketogenic diet provides ketone bodies as an alternative energy source via MCT1 transporters.

questions.question: An infant with refractory neonatal seizures is found to have elevated urine alpha-aminoadipic semialdehyde (AASA). After confirming biallelic ALDH7A1 variants, which treatment strategy is most likely to optimize neurodevelopmental outcomes?
options: Pyridoxine alone at standard dosing
Pyridoxal-5-phosphate (PLP), because ALDH7A1 deficiency specifically requires the active cofactor form
Ketogenic diet, as for all refractory neonatal seizures
Triple therapy: pyridoxine + lysine-restricted diet + arginine supplementation to reduce AASA accumulation
questions.explanation: Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy (PDE-ALDH7A1) is treated with lifelong pyridoxine, but intellectual disability occurs in ~75% even with seizure control. Triple therapy (pyridoxine + lysine restriction + arginine supplementation) reduces accumulation of the neurotoxic metabolite AASA/P6C and has improved cognitive outcomes in published series. PNPO deficiency (not ALDH7A1) requires PLP specifically.

questions.question: A 4-month-old infant presents with infantile spasms. MRI reveals cortical tubers, subependymal nodules, and a cardiac rhabdomyoma. Genetic testing confirms a pathogenic TSC2 variant. What is the recommended first-line antiepileptic treatment?
options: Everolimus, the mTOR inhibitor that directly targets the TSC molecular defect
Vigabatrin, which shows a ~95% response rate specifically in TSC-associated infantile spasms
ACTH, the universal first-line for all causes of infantile spasms
Levetiracetam, a broad-spectrum agent with favorable tolerability in infants
questions.explanation: Vigabatrin is the recommended first-line treatment specifically for TSC-associated infantile spasms, with ~95% response rate — far higher than in non-TSC infantile spasms (~50% for ACTH). This is a key exception to the general approach where ACTH is preferred.

questions.question: A TSC infant with epileptiform EEG but no clinical seizures is started on preventive vigabatrin based on the EPISTOP trial. Compared to conventionally treated infants, the EPISTOP trial demonstrated which outcome at 24 months?
options: Reduced incidence of epilepsy, reduced drug-resistant epilepsy, and improved neurodevelopmental outcomes
No significant difference compared to conventional treatment
Increased risk of retinal toxicity without seizure benefit
Benefit only in TSC1 (not TSC2) variant carriers
questions.explanation: The EPISTOP trial showed that preventive vigabatrin initiated before clinical seizures significantly reduced epilepsy incidence, drug-resistant epilepsy, and improved neurodevelopmental outcomes at 24 months. This established the concept of preventive antiepileptic treatment in TSC.

questions.question: A child with refractory epilepsy has exome sequencing that returns a heterozygous missense VUS in SCN2A. The seizures are reasonably controlled on levetiracetam. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start sodium channel blocker therapy based on the SCN2A association
Reclassify the VUS as likely pathogenic based on clinical correlation
Counsel the family that a VUS does not confirm a diagnosis, continue current management, and recommend parental segregation testing plus re-analysis in 1-2 years
Discontinue levetiracetam because it is ineffective in SCN2A-related epilepsy
questions.explanation: A VUS must never be used to guide treatment decisions or confirm a diagnosis. The appropriate approach is to explain the uncertainty, manage based on clinical findings, encourage family segregation testing to generate additional evidence (PP1/PS2), and schedule re-analysis in 1-2 years as databases grow.

questions.question: A heterozygous frameshift variant c.1234delA (p.Asp412ValfsTer19) is identified in SCN1A in a child with Dravet syndrome. SCN1A is a well-established haploinsufficiency gene. Which ACMG/AMP evidence criterion applies most directly?
options: PS3 — well-established functional study demonstrating a damaging effect
PM2 — absent from population databases
PP3 — multiple in silico tools predict a deleterious effect
PVS1 — null variant in a gene where loss of function is the established disease mechanism
questions.explanation: PVS1 (Very Strong pathogenic evidence) applies to null variants — nonsense, frameshift, canonical splice site, initiation codon, and whole exon deletions — in genes where loss of function is an established disease mechanism. SCN1A haploinsufficiency causes Dravet syndrome, making PVS1 directly applicable.

questions.question: A variant in a known autosomal dominant disease gene is found at an allele frequency of 7% in the East Asian subpopulation of gnomAD. Under ACMG/AMP criteria, what classification does this frequency support?
options: PM2 — Pathogenic Moderate, because the variant is rare overall
BA1 — Benign Stand-Alone, because the frequency exceeds 5% in a gnomAD subpopulation
BS1 — Benign Strong, suggesting the variant is more common than expected
PP3 — Pathogenic Supporting, based on population data alone
questions.explanation: BA1 (Benign Stand-Alone) is triggered when a variant's allele frequency exceeds 5% in any well-sampled gnomAD subpopulation. At 7%, this variant is far too common to cause a rare dominant disease. BA1 alone is sufficient to classify the variant as Benign.

questions.question: A laboratory classifies a variant in KCNQ2 using generic ACMG/AMP 2015 rules. However, a ClinGen Variant Curation Expert Panel (VCEP) has published gene-specific guidelines for this gene. What is the correct approach?
options: Use the VCEP guidelines only when the standard rules yield a VUS
Use VCEP guidelines as optional supplements alongside standard rules
Use VCEP guidelines only for novel variants not yet in ClinVar
VCEP gene-specific guidelines replace the standard ACMG/AMP rules and should always be used when available
questions.explanation: ClinGen VCEP gene-specific guidelines are developed by expert panels and approved by ClinGen. When published, they replace the generic ACMG/AMP 2015 rules for the specified gene, adjusting evidence thresholds, adding gene-specific criteria, and restricting inapplicable rules.

questions.question: A confirmed de novo missense variant is identified in a proband with a neurodevelopmental disorder. Neither parent carries the variant (trio sequencing confirmed). Which ACMG/AMP evidence criterion does this de novo status support?
options: PS2 — confirmed de novo in a proband with the disease and unaffected parents
PP1 — co-segregation with disease in affected family members
PM3 — detected in trans with a known pathogenic variant
PS4 — significantly more frequent in cases than controls
questions.explanation: PS2 (Strong pathogenic evidence) applies when a variant is confirmed de novo. De novo variants are particularly informative for autosomal dominant disorders and often tip classification from VUS to likely pathogenic when combined with additional criteria.

questions.question: A chromosomal microarray in a child with conotruncal heart defect, velopharyngeal insufficiency, and developmental delay reveals a 2.8 Mb deletion at 22q11.21. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Williams-Beuren syndrome (7q11.23 deletion)
1p36 deletion syndrome
22q11.2 deletion syndrome (DiGeorge/velocardiofacial syndrome)
Phelan-McDermid syndrome (22q13.3 deletion)
questions.explanation: The triad of conotruncal heart defect, palatal abnormality, and developmental delay is characteristic of 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (~1/4,000 births). This recurrent ~3 Mb deletion is mediated by NAHR between flanking segmental duplications.

questions.question: A child with intellectual disability has a chromosomal microarray showing a de novo 400 kb deletion encompassing a gene with ClinGen Haploinsufficiency score of 3. The child's phenotype matches the known gene-disease association. Under the ACMG/ClinGen CNV framework, this finding most strongly supports which classification?
options: Benign — the CNV is too small to be clinically significant
VUS — insufficient evidence without functional studies
Pathogenic — de novo status, HI score 3, and phenotype match provide strong multi-domain evidence
Likely Benign — inherited deletions of this gene are common
questions.explanation: Under the ACMG/ClinGen CNV framework, a de novo CNV encompassing a gene with established haploinsufficiency (HI score 3) and a matching clinical phenotype accumulates strong evidence across multiple domains. De novo CNVs are approximately 10-fold more likely to be pathogenic than inherited CNVs.

questions.question: A deletion at 15q11-13 is identified on the maternally inherited chromosome in a child with severe intellectual disability, absent speech, frequent laughing, and drug-resistant epilepsy. What is the diagnosis?
options: Prader-Willi syndrome due to loss of paternally expressed SNRPN
15q11-13 duplication syndrome causing autism spectrum disorder
Fragile X syndrome due to FMR1 silencing
Angelman syndrome due to loss of maternally expressed UBE3A
questions.explanation: A maternal deletion of 15q11-13 eliminates the only active copy of UBE3A (Angelman syndrome). In neurons, paternal UBE3A is silenced by UBE3A-ATS, so expression depends entirely on the maternal allele. A paternal deletion of the same region causes Prader-Willi syndrome — a classic example of genomic imprinting.

questions.question: Recurrent CNVs such as the 22q11.2 deletion and Williams syndrome deletion arise primarily through which mechanism?
options: Random double-strand breaks repaired by non-homologous end joining (NHEJ)
LINE-1 retrotransposon insertion causing focal DNA amplification
Non-allelic homologous recombination (NAHR) between flanking low-copy repeats during meiosis
Trinucleotide repeat expansion at unstable loci
questions.explanation: Recurrent genomic disorders arise through NAHR — meiotic recombination between paralogous segmental duplications flanking a genomic region. This produces deletions or duplications with consistent breakpoints at defined genomic loci.

questions.question: A 4-year-old boy presents with progressive proximal weakness, Gowers sign, calf pseudohypertrophy, and CK of 22,000 U/L. MLPA reveals a deletion of DMD exons 48-50 that disrupts the reading frame. What is the predicted phenotype?
options: Becker muscular dystrophy — partially functional truncated dystrophin is produced
Limb-girdle muscular dystrophy — DMD gene deletions do not cause dystrophinopathy
Spinal muscular atrophy — anterior horn cell disease mimicking myopathy
Duchenne muscular dystrophy — the out-of-frame deletion abolishes dystrophin production
questions.explanation: The reading-frame rule predicts that out-of-frame deletions abolish dystrophin production (Duchenne MD), while in-frame deletions produce truncated but partially functional dystrophin (Becker MD). This rule has ~90% accuracy. Exon 51-skipping therapy could potentially convert this out-of-frame deletion into an in-frame one.

questions.question: A newborn identified by newborn screening with absent SMN1 exon 7 is currently asymptomatic. SMN2 copy number is 2. Which statement about treatment is most accurate?
options: Presymptomatic treatment with gene replacement (onasemnogene abeparvovec) or antisense therapy (nusinersen) before motor neuron loss dramatically improves outcomes
Treatment should be delayed until clinical weakness appears to confirm the diagnosis
Only children with SMN2 copy number of 4 or more benefit from disease-modifying therapy
SMA is not treatable — only supportive care should be offered
questions.explanation: Multiple clinical trials demonstrate that presymptomatic SMA treatment yields dramatically better outcomes. Children treated presymptomatically often achieve nearly normal motor milestones. This is the primary rationale for SMA newborn screening (added to the US RUSP in 2018).

questions.question: A 25-year-old man has progressive distal leg weakness, high-arched feet, and hammertoes since childhood. His father has similar findings. Nerve conduction studies show uniform slowing of motor conduction velocity to 22 m/s in the median nerve. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: CMT2A (MFN2 mutation) — axonal neuropathy with reduced amplitudes and normal velocity
CMT1A (PMP22 duplication at 17p12) — demyelinating neuropathy with uniformly slow MCV, detected by MLPA
Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP) — PMP22 deletion
CMT1X (GJB1 mutation) — X-linked with intermediate conduction velocities
questions.explanation: The combination of autosomal dominant inheritance, childhood onset, distal weakness with pes cavus, and uniformly slow MCVs (22 m/s, well below the 38 m/s demyelinating threshold) is classic for CMT1A. PMP22 duplication on 17p12 accounts for ~40% of all CMT.

questions.question: A patient with Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy (LMNA mutation) has early elbow and ankle contractures but only mild limb weakness. What is the most critical surveillance recommendation?
options: Annual cardiac evaluation including ECG and echocardiogram due to high risk of conduction disease and sudden death
Annual pulmonary function testing for respiratory failure
Annual ophthalmologic examination for cataracts
Annual renal function monitoring for secondary renal disease
questions.explanation: The most dangerous complication of LMNA-related Emery-Dreifuss MD is cardiac involvement — progressive conduction disease, complete heart block, and dilated cardiomyopathy — which can cause sudden death even with minimal muscular weakness. Annual cardiac surveillance is essential.

questions.question: An 8-month-old boy with SMA type 1 is evaluated for gene therapy. His anti-AAV9 neutralizing antibody titer is 1:80. Which factor most significantly limits his eligibility for IV onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma)?
options: His SMN2 copy number of 2 predicts a phenotype too severe for gene therapy
AAV9 cannot cross the blood-brain barrier in children under 12 months
Only children with SMN2 copy number of 4 or more qualify for gene therapy
Pre-existing AAV9 neutralizing antibodies at titer >=1:50 block transduction and typically exclude patients from IV gene therapy
questions.explanation: Pre-existing AAV9 neutralizing antibodies (from natural AAV infection) are a key exclusion criterion for IV gene therapy. Titers >=1:50 block viral transduction, rendering treatment ineffective. All patients must be screened before enrollment. NAb prevalence increases with age (40-70% of adults are seropositive for AAV9).

questions.question: The cargo capacity of AAV vectors is approximately 4.7 kb. Why is this a significant challenge for Duchenne muscular dystrophy gene therapy?
options: The full-length dystrophin cDNA is ~14 kb, far exceeding AAV capacity, requiring engineered micro-dystrophin constructs that may not replicate all dystrophin functions
AAV vectors cannot transduce skeletal muscle tissue
The DMD gene promoter alone exceeds 4.7 kb
The DMD gene is X-linked, and AAV cannot deliver X-linked transgenes
questions.explanation: DMD encodes dystrophin with a 14 kb cDNA — far exceeding the 4.7 kb AAV limit. Micro-dystrophin constructs (~3.8 kb) retain key functional domains. Delandistrogene moxeparvovec (Elevidys), an AAVrh74-micro-dystrophin product, received accelerated FDA approval in 2023.

questions.question: A patient with SOD1-ALS on tofersen shows a substantial reduction in plasma neurofilament light (NfL) chain after 6 months. This biomarker change most directly indicates:
options: Successful immune activation against the ASO backbone
Complete clearance of SOD1 protein from motor neurons
Compensatory upregulation of other SOD isoforms
Reduced axonal injury and neurodegeneration, as NfL is released from damaged axons
questions.explanation: Neurofilament light chain is released from damaged neurons and axons. Elevated NfL reflects ongoing neurodegeneration. A sustained decrease after tofersen indicates reduced axonal injury — pharmacodynamic biomarker evidence that therapy is slowing neurodegeneration.

questions.question: A researcher asks which statement correctly distinguishes gene addition therapy from gene editing for neurological disorders. The correct answer is:
options: Gene addition delivers a functional gene copy that remains episomal in post-mitotic neurons; gene editing modifies the genomic DNA sequence at a specific locus
Gene addition integrates into the genome; gene editing uses episomal vectors
Gene editing is suitable only for recessive disorders; gene addition for dominant conditions
Both AAV gene addition and CRISPR gene editing permanently modify the patient's germline
questions.explanation: In gene addition (AAV-based therapy), the recombinant AAV genome remains largely episomal in post-mitotic neurons. Gene editing (CRISPR-Cas9) creates targeted modifications to the genomic DNA sequence. Neither modifies the germline — only somatic cells are treated.

questions.question: A 16-year-old presents with progressive gait ataxia since age 12, absent lower limb reflexes, loss of vibration sense, scoliosis, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. The most appropriate first-line diagnostic test is:
options: Comprehensive SCA repeat panel for SCA1-36
Whole exome sequencing as a first-tier test
Brain MRI with gadolinium to rule out posterior fossa mass
FXN GAA repeat expansion analysis by repeat-primed PCR — the definitive first-line test for Friedreich ataxia
questions.explanation: This presentation — teenage onset, ataxia, areflexia, sensory neuropathy, scoliosis, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy — is classic for Friedreich ataxia. FXN GAA repeat expansion testing (repeat-primed PCR) is the definitive first-line diagnostic test and directly detects biallelic expanded repeats (>66 GAA repeats in ~96–98% of patients). Standard exome sequencing does NOT detect GAA repeat expansions. Frataxin protein level (ELISA) is a supportive screening test but is not the definitive diagnostic assay.

questions.question: A 35-year-old man with progressive cerebellar ataxia, markedly slow saccadic eye movements, and peripheral neuropathy has a mother with similar symptoms. Brain MRI shows cerebellar and brainstem atrophy. Which spinocerebellar ataxia is most consistent?
options: SCA6 — pure cerebellar ataxia without neuropathy
SCA7 — characterized by progressive macular degeneration
SCA1 — characterized by hyperreflexia and pyramidal signs
SCA2 — characterized by slow saccades, cerebellar atrophy, and peripheral neuropathy
questions.explanation: SCA2 (ATXN2) is strongly suggested by cerebellar ataxia, markedly slow saccades (a cardinal early feature), and peripheral neuropathy with AD inheritance. Intermediate ATXN2 CAG repeats (27-33) are also an ALS risk factor.

questions.question: A father with SCA3 has a son who develops symptoms 15 years earlier. This earlier onset is best explained by:
options: Genetic anticipation — the CAG repeat expanded further during paternal transmission
Environmental factors accelerating the disease
A de novo second pathogenic variant in ATXN3
Somatic mosaicism for the repeat expansion in the son's cerebellum
questions.explanation: Genetic anticipation is a hallmark of CAG repeat SCAs. During spermatogenesis, the expanded CAG repeat tends to further increase in length, resulting in earlier onset and greater severity in the next generation.

questions.question: A 25-year-old woman presents with recurrent stroke-like episodes with posterior-predominant MRI changes crossing vascular territories, lactic acidosis, sensorineural hearing loss, and maternal relatives with diabetes and deafness. The most likely diagnosis is:
options: CADASIL — autosomal dominant NOTCH3 arteriopathy
MELAS syndrome, most commonly caused by m.3243A>G in MT-TL1
Antiphospholipid syndrome — autoimmune thrombotic disorder
MERRF syndrome — caused by m.8344A>G in MT-TK
questions.explanation: This is classic MELAS: stroke-like episodes with posterior-predominant cortical MRI lesions crossing vascular territories, lactic acidosis, maternal diabetes + deafness, and hearing loss. The m.3243A>G variant in MT-TL1 accounts for ~80% of MELAS. MELAS lesions are caused by focal metabolic failure, not vascular occlusion.

questions.question: A woman with 40% m.3243A>G heteroplasmy in blood wants to know the risk for her future children. Which counseling statement is most accurate?
options: 50% of her children will inherit the variant with identical 40% heteroplasmy
None of her children will be affected because 40% is below the disease threshold
Because mtDNA is maternal, all children will inherit some copies, but their specific heteroplasmy levels are unpredictable due to the mitochondrial bottleneck
Male children cannot be affected because mtDNA disease follows X-linked inheritance
questions.explanation: All children of a heteroplasmic mother inherit some mutant mtDNA. The mitochondrial bottleneck creates large, unpredictable heteroplasmy shifts. A mother with 40% may have children ranging from <5% to >90%, making counseling extremely challenging.

questions.question: A patient with suspected mitochondrial epilepsy is started on valproate and develops acute liver failure. This complication is most strongly associated with variants in which gene?
options: SCN1A — sodium channel mutations increase valproate hepatotoxicity risk
POLG — polymerase gamma variants cause valproate-induced hepatotoxicity and are an absolute contraindication
MT-TL1 — MELAS patients are uniquely susceptible to valproate liver injury
UGT1A6 — impaired glucuronidation of valproate causes toxic accumulation
questions.explanation: Valproate-induced hepatotoxicity (Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome) is most strongly associated with biallelic POLG variants. POLG testing should be performed before valproate for epileptic encephalopathy with developmental regression or mitochondrial features. POLG variants are an absolute contraindication to valproate.

questions.question: A child presents with bilateral symmetric T2 hyperintensity on MRI involving the putamen, thalami, and periaqueductal gray matter, with elevated plasma lactate. This MRI pattern is most consistent with:
options: Leigh syndrome (subacute necrotizing encephalopathy) — bilateral symmetric basal ganglia and brainstem involvement
MELAS — posterior-predominant stroke-like lesions crossing vascular territories
Kearns-Sayre syndrome — progressive external ophthalmoplegia and pigmentary retinopathy
MERRF — diffuse white matter T2 signal abnormality
questions.explanation: Bilateral symmetric T2 hyperintensity in basal ganglia (putamen), thalami, and brainstem (periaqueductal gray) with elevated lactate is the defining MRI pattern of Leigh syndrome, the most common mitochondrial disease in childhood, caused by variants in over 75 genes.

questions.question: A 2-year-old girl who developed normally begins losing purposeful hand skills, stops using her 10-word vocabulary, develops repetitive hand-wringing movements, and shows decelerating head growth. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Angelman syndrome — happy demeanor, seizures, and absent speech from infancy
Fragile X syndrome — the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability
Rett syndrome (MECP2) — regression with loss of hand skills and speech, hand stereotypies, and acquired microcephaly
Dravet syndrome — seizure-onset epileptic encephalopathy
questions.explanation: This is classic Rett syndrome Stage II (Rapid Regression): a previously normally developing girl loses hand skills and spoken language, develops stereotypic hand-wringing, and shows acquired microcephaly. Rett affects almost exclusively females and is caused by de novo MECP2 variants in >95% of cases.

questions.question: A gene therapy team developing AAV9-MECP2 for Rett syndrome faces a unique challenge not encountered with SMN1 gene therapy for SMA. What is this challenge?
options: MECP2 cDNA is too large to fit in an AAV vector
AAV9 cannot transduce neurons in the central nervous system
MECP2 is dosage-sensitive — both underexpression (Rett) and overexpression (MECP2 duplication syndrome) cause severe disease, requiring precise dosing
MECP2 is located on an autosome, preventing AAV delivery
questions.explanation: MECP2 is dosage-sensitive: loss of function causes Rett, while duplication causes MECP2 duplication syndrome. Gene therapy must achieve expression neither too low nor too high — a narrow therapeutic window. MECP2 cDNA is only ~1.5 kb (well within AAV capacity); the challenge is precise dosing, not vector size.

questions.question: A 10-year-old boy with moderate intellectual disability, anxiety, prominent ears, long face, and joint hypermobility is evaluated. His maternal grandfather has progressive tremor and gait ataxia, and his mother had premature menopause at age 36. What is the correct first test?
options: Whole exome sequencing for comprehensive coding variant analysis
FMR1 CGG repeat analysis — the features and family history suggest Fragile X with premutation-associated FXTAS and FXPOI
Chromosomal microarray to detect copy number variants
MECP2 sequencing for a Rett-like disorder in a male
questions.explanation: This family shows the full FMR1 spectrum: the boy has Fragile X features (full mutation), the grandfather has FXTAS (premutation: tremor + ataxia), and the mother has FXPOI (premutation: premature menopause). FMR1 CGG repeat analysis is the correct test — standard WES does NOT detect CGG repeat expansions (modern WGS may screen for some STR disorders but dedicated repeat analysis remains the gold standard).

questions.question: A 3-year-old boy presents with absent speech, frequent laughter, hand-flapping, wide-based ataxic gait, and seizures. EEG shows characteristic high-amplitude 2–3 Hz delta activity with superimposed spikes. Methylation analysis of 15q11-13 shows only the paternal methylation pattern (absent maternal band). Which mechanism is most likely responsible?
options: Paternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 15
Maternal deletion of 15q11-13 (~70% of cases) — loss of the maternally expressed UBE3A gene, which is the only active copy in neurons due to tissue-specific paternal imprinting
Biallelic UBE3A point variants inherited from both parents
Paternal deletion of 15q11-13 causing loss of SNRPN expression
questions.explanation: This is classic Angelman syndrome: absent speech, happy demeanor with frequent laughter, ataxia, seizures, and characteristic EEG. Methylation analysis showing only the paternal pattern confirms loss of maternal 15q11-13 contribution. The most common mechanism (~70%) is maternal deletion. UBE3A is subject to tissue-specific genomic imprinting: in neurons, the paternal UBE3A allele is silenced by an antisense transcript, making UBE3A expression exclusively maternal. Loss of the maternal copy therefore abolishes neuronal UBE3A protein. Paternal UPD15 (~2%) also causes Angelman but through having two paternal (silenced) copies rather than deletion.

questions.question: A 28-year-old woman whose father died of Huntington disease requests predictive testing. She has no symptoms. According to the international HD predictive testing protocol, which is required before testing?
options: A minimum of two pre-test counseling sessions with psychological assessment and identification of a support person
Concurrent testing of her siblings to establish family-wide risk
A brain MRI to rule out presymptomatic structural changes
Written consent from her father's neurologist confirming the familial diagnosis
questions.explanation: The international HD predictive testing protocol requires minimum two counseling sessions with a cooling-off period, psychological assessment for depression and suicidality, and a support person. This rigorous framework reflects the profound psychological impact of a positive result for a currently untreatable neurodegenerative disease.

questions.question: Parents of a healthy 5-year-old boy whose grandfather has Huntington disease request predictive HD testing for their son. What is the most appropriate response?
options: Proceed with testing because the parents have legal authority to consent
Offer testing only if the child verbally assents
Perform exclusion testing using linkage analysis
Decline testing, explaining that predictive testing for adult-onset conditions without childhood intervention should be deferred until the child can provide autonomous informed consent
questions.explanation: ACMG and AAP guidelines recommend deferring predictive testing for adult-onset conditions without childhood medical actionability until the individual can provide autonomous consent (typically age 18). Testing this child removes his future right to decide whether to know his status.

questions.question: A man receives a positive presymptomatic genetic test result for a neurological condition. He applies for life insurance in the United States. Under the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act (GINA), which statement is correct?
options: GINA prohibits all insurance companies from using genetic results in underwriting
GINA protects against discrimination by health insurers and employers but does NOT protect in life, disability, or long-term care insurance
GINA applies only to individuals with a confirmed clinical diagnosis
GINA requires genetic test results to be sealed for 10 years
questions.explanation: GINA (2008) prohibits genetic discrimination by health insurers and employers. However, it does NOT extend to life, disability, or long-term care insurance. Patients must be informed of this critical gap during pre-test counseling.

questions.question: Both parents are confirmed carriers of SMN1 deletion (SMA carriers). They wish to avoid prenatal diagnosis with possible termination. Which reproductive option best addresses their preference?
options: Preimplantation genetic testing for monogenic disorders (PGT-M) with IVF, allowing selection of unaffected embryos before pregnancy
Non-invasive prenatal screening (NIPS) for SMA
Expanded carrier rescreening of both parents
Natural conception with plan for immediate neonatal gene therapy
questions.explanation: PGT-M with IVF allows testing embryos for familial SMN1 deletions at blastocyst stage, transferring only unaffected or carrier embryos. This directly addresses the couple's preference to avoid prenatal diagnosis and potential termination.

questions.question: A 3-year-old boy has hyperphagia, obesity, hypotonia since infancy, and mild intellectual disability. SNRPN methylation analysis shows only the methylated (maternal) band. Which mechanism is LEAST likely to explain this result?
options: Imprinting center defect causing abnormal methylation of the paternal allele
Maternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 15
Paternal deletion of 15q11-13
A point variant in UBE3A
questions.explanation: The SNRPN methylation pattern showing only the maternal band indicates loss of paternal 15q11-13 expression (Prader-Willi syndrome), caused by paternal deletion, maternal UPD15, or IC defect. A UBE3A point variant causes Angelman syndrome (loss of maternal expression) and would show normal SNRPN methylation.

questions.question: A clinician suspects Kabuki syndrome in a child with intellectual disability, characteristic facial features, and congenital anomalies, but KMT2D and KDM6A sequencing are negative. What additional test could identify the diagnosis?
options: Frataxin protein level, as Kabuki is associated with repeat expansion
Genome-wide methylation array for episignature analysis — chromatin regulator dysfunction produces a characteristic methylation fingerprint even without a detectable sequence variant
Mitochondrial DNA sequencing, as Kabuki has a maternal inheritance pattern
Chromosomal microarray to detect a deletion at the KMT2D locus
questions.explanation: Disorders caused by dysfunctional chromatin regulators produce reproducible genome-wide methylation episignatures. Over 50 disorders have defined episignatures, enabling diagnosis when standard sequencing is non-diagnostic. This is one of the most clinically useful applications of episignature analysis.

questions.question: A 6-month-old boy presents with infantile spasms (West syndrome). EEG shows hypsarrhythmia. Brain MRI is normal. Chromosomal microarray reveals a 1.5 Mb deletion at 1p36. Which statement about this finding is most accurate?
options: The deletion is likely benign because the MRI is normal

1p36 deletion syndrome is a recognized cause of epilepsy including infantile spasms, intellectual disability, and distinctive features — this is a clinically significant finding explaining the presentation
This deletion causes Angelman syndrome through disruption of UBE3A
A 1.5 Mb deletion is below the resolution of CMA and should be confirmed by FISH
questions.explanation: 1p36 deletion syndrome is one of the most common subtelomeric deletion syndromes (~1 in 5,000 births) and is a well-recognized cause of epilepsy — including infantile spasms — along with intellectual disability, hypotonia, distinctive features, and cardiomyopathy. A normal MRI does not exclude a genetic etiology for infantile spasms; CMA is recommended in the workup and can identify causative CNVs in ~7-10% of cases. A 1.5 Mb deletion is well above CMA resolution (~50-100 kb). This underscores the importance of genetic testing in infantile spasms even when neuroimaging is unrevealing.

questions.question: A pathogenic variant c.247C>T (p.Arg83Ter) is identified in exon 3 of a gene with 10 exons. What is the predicted molecular consequence?
options: The mRNA will likely undergo nonsense-mediated decay (NMD), resulting in reduced or absent protein
A stable truncated protein of 83 amino acids will be produced
The variant is synonymous with no functional effect
The variant causes in-frame skipping of exon 3
questions.explanation: A premature termination codon in exon 3 of a 10-exon gene triggers NMD (the PTC is >50-55 nt upstream of the final exon-exon junction). NMD degrades the abnormal mRNA, resulting in loss of function — the basis for applying PVS1.

questions.question: A patient has an unexplained genetic condition. Exome sequencing identifies only a synonymous variant (c.300G>A, p.Thr100=) in a strong candidate gene. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Synonymous variants cannot cause disease because the amino acid is unchanged
This should be classified as Benign without further analysis
Synonymous variants only affect translation rate without clinical consequence
The variant may disrupt an exonic splicing enhancer (ESE), causing exon skipping, and warrants RNA-level functional assessment
questions.explanation: Synonymous variants can disrupt exonic splicing enhancer sequences, causing exon skipping even though the amino acid is unchanged. RNA-level studies should be considered before assuming a synonymous variant in a strong candidate gene is benign.

questions.question: The full mutation CGG repeat expansion in FMR1 (>200 repeats) causes Fragile X syndrome primarily through which mechanism?
options: Toxic gain-of-function polyglutamine protein accumulation
Epigenetic silencing: hypermethylation of the expanded CGG repeat and FMR1 promoter, abolishing FMRP production
Chromosomal instability causing frequent breaks at Xq27.3
The CGG repeat alters the FMR1 coding sequence, producing a dysfunctional protein
questions.explanation: Full mutation alleles trigger hypermethylation of the CGG repeat and the CpG island promoter, silencing FMR1 transcription and abolishing FMRP. The repeat is in the 5' UTR, not the coding sequence. This epigenetic silencing distinguishes Fragile X from coding repeat expansion disorders.

questions.question: A 14-year-old boy presents with myoclonic jerks of the arms and mild dystonic posturing of the neck since age 8. His symptoms dramatically improve after alcohol consumption. His father has similar jerky movements. Brain MRI is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy — myoclonus with photosensitivity
Wilson disease — dystonia with hepatic involvement
Myoclonus-dystonia (DYT-SGCE) — autosomal dominant with maternal imprinting; characterized by myoclonus, dystonia, and alcohol responsiveness
Essential tremor — postural and action tremor with alcohol responsiveness
questions.explanation: Myoclonus-dystonia (DYT11, SGCE) presents with childhood-onset myoclonus (predominantly upper body) and mild dystonia, with characteristic dramatic improvement after alcohol. SGCE is maternally imprinted — the disorder manifests only when the pathogenic variant is inherited from the father (maternal allele is silenced). This imprinting pattern explains why an affected father transmits the phenotype but an affected mother's children are usually unaffected. Psychiatric comorbidities (OCD, anxiety, alcohol dependence) are common. Essential tremor also improves with alcohol but presents with postural/action tremor rather than myoclonus.

questions.question: A young adult with dystonia, liver disease, and Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit-lamp examination has low ceruloplasmin. The correct diagnosis and treatment are:
options: DRD (GCH1) — treat with levodopa/carbidopa
Wilson disease (ATP7B) — treat with copper chelation (penicillamine or trientine) and zinc
Friedreich ataxia — treat with omaveloxolone
Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (PKAN) — treat with pantothenate
questions.explanation: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive copper metabolism disorder (ATP7B mutations). Kayser-Fleischer rings are pathognomonic. Wilson disease is fully treatable with copper chelation and must be excluded in any young patient with dystonia + liver disease.

questions.question: A 38-year-old woman presents with recurrent subcortical lacunar strokes, migraine with aura since age 28, and a father who had dementia and strokes in his 50s. MRI shows periventricular white matter changes with anterior temporal lobe and external capsule involvement. The most likely diagnosis is:
options: CADASIL — autosomal dominant NOTCH3 arteriopathy with characteristic anterior temporal and external capsule white matter changes
Fabry disease — alpha-galactosidase A deficiency
Antiphospholipid syndrome — autoimmune thrombotic disorder
MELAS — mitochondrial stroke-like episodes
questions.explanation: CADASIL is the most common hereditary stroke disorder in adults, caused by cysteine-altering NOTCH3 variants. The clinical tetrad includes migraine with aura, recurrent lacunar strokes, psychiatric disturbance, and progressive cognitive decline. The MRI signature of anterior temporal and external capsule involvement is characteristic.

questions.question: A Han Chinese patient with new-onset epilepsy is being considered for carbamazepine. What screening test is recommended before prescribing?
options: CYP2C9 genotyping to predict carbamazepine metabolism
SCN1A sequencing to confirm sodium channel sensitivity
HLA-B*15:02 genotyping to assess risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis
CYP3A4 activity testing to predict carbamazepine clearance
questions.explanation: HLA-B*15:02 is present in ~8-10% of Han Chinese and Southeast Asian populations. It confers >10-fold increased risk of carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN. The FDA requires HLA-B*15:02 testing before carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine in patients with ancestry from high-risk populations.

questions.question: A clinically unaffected couple has a child with a severe de novo autosomal dominant condition. Parental blood sequencing confirms both parents are negative. Their second child is born with the same condition. Which explanation best accounts for this recurrence?
options: The mutation spontaneously recurred independently in the second child
Both parents are obligate carriers of an autosomal recessive form
The couple's shared environmental exposure caused the same mutation
One parent has germline (gonadal) mosaicism — the variant is present in germ cells but absent or undetectable in blood
questions.explanation: Germline mosaicism explains recurrence of apparently de novo conditions. The variant exists in a parent's germ cells but is absent or below detection in blood. Germline mosaicism cannot be detected by standard blood sequencing and accounts for 1-4% empirical recurrence risk. PGT-M and prenatal diagnosis should be offered.

questions.question: A 14-year-old with progressive cerebellar ataxia and sensory neuropathy has a negative standard whole exome sequencing result. Which is the most critical next step?
options: Repeat the WES at a different laboratory
Accept the negative result as final — the ataxia is likely acquired
Order dedicated repeat expansion testing (repeat-primed PCR for Friedreich ataxia, SCA types, CANVAS) — the most common hereditary ataxias are NOT detected by standard WES
Proceed directly to brain biopsy
questions.explanation: The most common hereditary ataxias are caused by repeat expansions not detected by standard WES (modern WGS may screen for some STR disorders but with variable sensitivity): Friedreich ataxia (FXN GAA), SCAs (CAG repeats), and CANVAS (RFC1 AAGGG repeat, which causes ataxia + sensory neuropathy matching this presentation). A negative WES is NOT diagnostic closure — dedicated repeat testing must be ordered.

questions.question: A 4-year-old born at term without perinatal complications has been labeled with 'spastic diplegic cerebral palsy.' Brain MRI is normal. The parents report that her tone and walking are notably worse by evening and improved after sleeping. Which is the most important next step?
options: Chromosomal microarray to identify a pathogenic copy number variant
A therapeutic trial of low-dose levodopa/carbidopa to evaluate for dopa-responsive dystonia
Botulinum toxin injections to the spastic lower extremities
Exome sequencing with trio analysis to detect a de novo variant
questions.explanation: Diurnal fluctuation of tone (worse at end of day, improved after sleep) is the hallmark of dopa-responsive dystonia (DRD, GCH1). DRD is a critical treatable mimic of cerebral palsy — it responds dramatically and sustainedly to very low doses of levodopa (25–50 mg/day) without motor fluctuations. A levodopa trial should always be performed before accepting a CP diagnosis in any child with dystonic features or diurnal fluctuation. Missing this diagnosis condemns a child to progressive disability from a fully treatable condition.

questions.question: A term-born child with spastic quadriplegia, epilepsy, and intellectual disability has been diagnosed with cerebral palsy. Brain MRI shows bilateral polymicrogyria. There is no history of perinatal hypoxic-ischemic injury. Which statement best characterizes the role of genetic testing?
options: Genetic testing is unnecessary because the MRI finding of polymicrogyria confirms an acquired (vascular) etiology
Only karyotype is indicated — microarray and exome have no role in CP evaluation

Chromosomal microarray and exome sequencing should be pursued — genetic causes account for 20–30% of CP cases, with highest yield in term births with cortical malformations and no clear perinatal etiology
Genetic testing should be deferred until adulthood when the child can consent
questions.explanation: Modern evidence demonstrates that genetic causes — including pathogenic CNVs, single-gene variants, and chromosomal abnormalities — underlie approximately 20–30% of CP cases, with the highest diagnostic yield in term-born children without perinatal risk factors, children with cortical malformations (like polymicrogyria), and children with additional features beyond motor impairment. Bilateral symmetric polymicrogyria strongly suggests a genetic etiology (TUBB2B, ADGRG1 [formerly GPR56]). Genetic diagnosis informs recurrence risk counseling, comorbidity surveillance, and potential targeted therapy.

questions.question: A cytogenetics report describes a karyotype as 45,X[12]/46,XX[18]. Which interpretation is correct?
options: A female with trisomy X — 47,XXX in all cells
A male with Klinefelter syndrome mosaicism
A female with mosaic Turner syndrome — 12 of 30 cells show monosomy X (45,X) and 18 show a normal female complement (46,XX)
A structurally abnormal X chromosome with a ring formation
questions.explanation: ISCN mosaic notation lists each cell line followed by the cell count in brackets: 45,X[12]/46,XX[18] indicates 12 cells with monosomy X and 18 cells with normal 46,XX, out of 30 total metaphases analyzed. This is mosaic Turner syndrome, which accounts for 15–20% of Turner syndrome cases. Mosaic Turner patients often have milder ovarian insufficiency and fewer somatic features than constitutional 45,X, though phenotype cannot be reliably predicted from the blood mosaicism percentage alone.

questions.question: A 15-year-old boy with a known 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge syndrome) presents with 6 months of social withdrawal, declining grades, paranoid ideation, and auditory hallucinations. His parents attribute these changes to 'teenage behavior.' What is the most appropriate response?
options: Reassure the family — behavioral changes in adolescence are expected and usually self-limited in 22q11.2DS
Start methylphenidate for ADHD, the most common psychiatric comorbidity in 22q11.2DS
Urgent psychiatric referral for evaluation of prodromal or first-episode psychosis — 22q11.2DS carries a 25–30% lifetime risk of schizophrenia, the highest known single genetic risk factor
Order brain MRI to evaluate for a structural cause of hallucinations before considering psychiatric referral
questions.explanation: 22q11.2 deletion syndrome carries a 25–30% lifetime risk of schizophrenia-spectrum disorders — the single highest genetic risk factor for psychosis. Prodromal symptoms (social withdrawal, paranoid thinking, negative symptoms) typically emerge in adolescence. This presentation requires urgent psychiatric evaluation, not reassurance. Diagnostic overshadowing — attributing psychiatric symptoms to the underlying genetic condition or to 'behavioral issues' — is a major barrier to timely treatment in dual-diagnosis populations.

questions.question: A 3-year-old with progressive white matter disease on MRI shows dramatic worsening after a mild febrile illness. MRI demonstrates diffuse white matter rarefaction approaching CSF signal intensity on FLAIR, with relative preservation of subcortical U-fibers. Which diagnosis is most likely?
options: X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy — posterior-predominant white matter involvement with enhancing leading edge
Metachromatic leukodystrophy — confluent periventricular white matter disease with tigroid pattern
Vanishing white matter disease (EIF2B-related) — episodic deterioration triggered by fever or minor trauma, with white matter rarefaction approaching CSF signal
Alexander disease — frontal-predominant white matter disease with macrocephaly
questions.explanation: Vanishing white matter disease (VWM), caused by biallelic variants in EIF2B1-5 (encoding subunits of the eIF2B translation initiation factor), is characterized by progressive white matter rarefaction where affected white matter approaches CSF signal intensity on FLAIR and diffusion imaging. A distinctive feature is episodic deterioration triggered by fever, minor head trauma, or emotional stress. Preservation of subcortical U-fibers early in the disease is characteristic. The other leukodystrophies listed have distinct MRI patterns: ALD shows posterior-predominant disease with an enhancing leading edge, MLD shows a tigroid pattern, and Alexander disease is frontal-predominant.

questions.question: An infant presents with hypotonia, oculomotor apraxia, and episodic hyperpnea. Axial MRI shows a distinctive 'molar tooth sign' with thickened, horizontally oriented superior cerebellar peduncles and a deepened interpeduncular fossa. Which diagnosis does this confirm, and what surveillance is essential?
options: Dandy-Walker malformation; surveillance for hydrocephalus only
Joubert syndrome (ciliopathy); mandatory screening for retinal dystrophy, nephronophthisis, and hepatic fibrosis
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia; referral for genetic testing of TSEN complex genes
Rhombencephalosynapsis; no specific surveillance beyond developmental monitoring
questions.explanation: The molar tooth sign on axial MRI is pathognomonic for Joubert syndrome, a ciliopathy with extreme genetic heterogeneity (>40 genes). Beyond the neurological features (hypotonia, oculomotor apraxia, episodic hyperpnea), Joubert syndrome requires mandatory surveillance for retinal dystrophy, nephronophthisis (renal disease), and hepatic fibrosis — multi-organ complications whose presence and severity vary by genotype. Missing renal surveillance can lead to undetected progressive kidney disease.

questions.question: A 6-year-old undergoes epilepsy surgery for drug-resistant focal seizures. Standard blood-based exome sequencing was previously negative. Deep sequencing (1000× depth) of the resected focal cortical dysplasia tissue reveals a somatic MTOR activating variant at 8% variant allele fraction. Which statement best explains this finding?
options: The variant is an artifact — somatic variants at 8% VAF are below the detection limit of all current technologies
This is a germline variant that was missed by exome due to poor coverage of the MTOR gene

This is brain somatic mosaicism — a postzygotic MTOR activating variant arising in neural progenitor cells, causing focal cortical dysplasia through localized mTOR pathway activation; it was absent from blood and required deep tissue sequencing for detection
The variant must also be present in blood at 8% but was missed due to standard exome depth (~100×)
questions.explanation: Brain somatic mosaicism — mutations arising in neural progenitor cells during cortical development — is now recognized as a major cause of focal cortical dysplasia type II. Somatic activating mutations in the mTOR pathway (MTOR, PIK3CA, AKT3, TSC1/TSC2) are found at low VAF (often 1–20%) in surgically resected FCD tissue but are typically absent from blood. Standard clinical exome sequencing (50–100× depth) cannot reliably detect variants below 10–15% VAF, and blood testing misses brain-restricted somatic mutations entirely. Deep targeted sequencing of affected tissue is required.

questions.question: A 32-year-old man presents with a cryptogenic posterior circulation ischemic stroke. He reports intermittent burning pain in his hands and feet since adolescence. Examination reveals scattered angiokeratomas and corneal verticillata on slit-lamp exam. Which diagnosis should be urgently evaluated, and why?
options: CADASIL — NOTCH3 testing should be ordered based on the young age of stroke onset
Fabry disease (GLA) — alpha-galactosidase A enzyme activity should be measured immediately because enzyme replacement therapy is available and can prevent further organ damage
Homocystinuria — plasma homocysteine should be measured based on the young stroke presentation
Factor V Leiden — thrombophilia testing is the most urgent next step for any cryptogenic young stroke
questions.explanation: The combination of cryptogenic posterior circulation stroke in a young adult, acroparesthesias (burning extremity pain from small fiber neuropathy), angiokeratomas, and corneal verticillata is classic for Fabry disease — an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder caused by alpha-galactosidase A (GLA) deficiency. Fabry disease is an urgent diagnosis because enzyme replacement therapy (agalsidase alfa or beta) and oral chaperone therapy (migalastat) are available and can prevent progressive renal, cardiac, and cerebrovascular damage. Screening young cryptogenic stroke patients for Fabry is increasingly recommended.

questions.question: A 10-year-old Ashkenazi Jewish boy presents with progressive dystonia that began in his right foot at age 7 and has generalized over the past 2 years. Brain MRI is normal. He has failed trials of levodopa and trihexyphenidyl. Genetic testing reveals a heterozygous TOR1A GAG deletion (ΔE303). Which treatment offers the best chance of sustained improvement?
options: High-dose trihexyphenidyl (up to 60 mg/day) with slow titration
Botulinum toxin injections to the most affected muscle groups
Deep brain stimulation (DBS) of the globus pallidus internus (GPi), which achieves 50–90% sustained improvement in DYT-TOR1A dystonia
Intrathecal baclofen pump, the standard of care for generalized genetic dystonia
questions.explanation: DYT-TOR1A is the most common early-onset primary generalized dystonia, with a founder effect in Ashkenazi Jewish populations (~16% of early-onset dystonia in this group). When medical therapy fails, GPi deep brain stimulation is the treatment of choice, achieving 50–90% sustained improvement — particularly in young patients before fixed postures develop. DYT-TOR1A is one of the most DBS-responsive genetic dystonias. The TOR1A GAG deletion has ~30–40% penetrance, with onset before age 26 in affected individuals.

questions.question: A child with intellectual disability, autism spectrum disorder, and distinctive features has a negative chromosomal microarray (CMA). The geneticist recommends exome sequencing. The parents ask why CMA was not sufficient. Which explanation is most accurate?
options: CMA was a waste of resources — exome should always be the first-tier test for neurodevelopmental disorders

CMA detects copy number variants (deletions/duplications ≥50–100 kb) but cannot detect single nucleotide variants or small insertions/deletions in individual genes; exome sequencing complements CMA by detecting these variant classes, with an additional diagnostic yield of ~25–30% after negative CMA
CMA only works for chromosomal aneuploidies like trisomy 21 and cannot detect any sub-microscopic changes
Exome sequencing detects everything CMA does plus more — CMA is now obsolete as a first-tier test
questions.explanation: CMA and exome sequencing are complementary tests. CMA detects copy number variants (diagnostic yield ~10–15% in ID/ASD) but cannot detect sequence-level changes in single genes. Exome sequencing detects single nucleotide variants and small indels (additional yield ~25–30% after negative CMA) but has limited sensitivity for CNVs, especially balanced rearrangements and low-level mosaicism. Both tests are part of the tiered genetic evaluation for neurodevelopmental disorders. CMA remains a recommended first-tier test alongside exome in many guidelines.
